Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First, I need to understand what a process variant is. According to the problem, its the ordered sequence of activities for a single case, sorted by timestamp. So for each case_id, I have to collect all its activities in the order they happened based on timestamp to form a sequence. Then group cases by these sequences to find unique variants.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: In SQL, to aggregate ordered lists, you can use LIST or STRING_AGG. But since the order matters, we need to make sure that within each case, the activities are ordered by timestamp before aggregating. So first step: For each case, sort the activities by timestamp, then aggregate them into a list or a string.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: SELECT case_id, LIST(activity ORDER BY timestamp) AS activity_sequence FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id Yes, DuckDB supports LIST with ORDER BY. So that would give for each case_id, a list of activities in the order of timestamp.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: WITH case_sequences AS ( SELECT case_id, LIST(activity ORDER BY timestamp) AS variant FROM event_log GROUP BY case_id ), variant_counts AS ( SELECT variant, COUNT(*) AS case_count FROM case_sequences GROUP BY variant ORDER BY case_count DESC LIMIT K ) top_case_ids AS ( SELECT DISTINCT case_id FROM case_sequences WHERE variant IN (SELECT variant FROM variant_counts) ) Finally, select all events from event_log where case_id is in top_case_ids

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: K is a parameter here. The problem says top K variants, so the query needs to have K as a variable. In DuckDB, you can use a parameter like $K, but maybe the user expects the query to have a placeholder.

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: In the top_case_ids CTE, we have DISTINCT case_id, but since case_id is already unique in case_variants because case_variants is grouped by case_id, the DISTINCT is redundant. So we can remove DISTINCT.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Suppose K=2. Case 1 has variant A B, case 2 has A B, case3 has A C, case4 has A C, case5 has B A. Then case_variants would have two with A B, two with A C, one with B A. top_variants would be the top 2 variants: A B and A C. Then the final query would return all events from cases 1,2,3,4. Which is correct.

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: In DuckDB, the list type can be compared for equality, so grouping by the list will group cases with the same sequence. DuckDB supports grouping by list types.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Another edge case: if K is larger than the number of variants, then LIMIT K will just return all variants. Thats acceptable. Another point: if there are ties in count, LIMIT K takes the first K after ordering.

Name: Validation - PE
Text: I think this query meets all the requirements. The query correctly sorts by timestamp for each case. Returns all events from the original event_log for top cases.
